title: R-1 Setback Requirements (Typical)
text: Typical single-family residential (R-1) zoning setbacks: front yard 20-25 feet; interior side yard 5 feet (10 feet on the street side of corner lots); rear yard 15-25 feet. Setbacks measured from the property line to the nearest point of the structure. Eaves, chimneys, and bay windows may project into setbacks subject to limits (typically 24-36 inches). Local zoning ordinance governs exact values.

title: Maximum Building Height (Typical)
text: Typical maximum building height in single-family zones: 28 to 35 feet, measured from existing or finished grade (whichever is lower) to the highest point of the roof. Some jurisdictions measure to the midpoint of a sloped roof. Hillside overlays may impose lower limits or step-back requirements. Verify exact measurement methodology with the local zoning ordinance.

title: Floor Area Ratio (Typical)
text: Floor Area Ratio (FAR) = total gross building floor area divided by the lot area. Typical single-family residential FAR limits range from 0.45 to 0.60. Larger lots may step down (anti-mansionization). Garages, basements, and attics may be partially excluded. Definitions vary by jurisdiction; confirm method of measurement (gross vs. net, included spaces).

title: Lot Coverage (Typical)
text: Maximum lot coverage by buildings and structures in single-family residential zones typically ranges from 30 to 50 percent. Lot coverage generally measured as the area of the lot covered by the principal building, accessory structures, and covered patios/decks. Some jurisdictions separately limit impervious surface (driveways, walks) at higher percentages (50-70%).

title: Parking Requirements (Typical)
text: Single-family dwellings typically require 2 off-street parking spaces, at least 1 in a covered structure (garage or carport). Each space minimum 9x19 feet (standard) or 10x20 feet in some jurisdictions. Garage doors with vehicle access from a public street typically must be set back at least 18-20 feet from the property line.

title: Accessory Dwelling Unit Standards
text: California law requires that local agencies allow at least one accessory dwelling unit and one junior accessory dwelling unit on lots zoned for single-family or multifamily use. Maximum size limits cannot be set below 850 sf for studios/1-bedroom or 1,000 sf for 2+ bedrooms. Maximum 4 ft side and rear setback required. No replacement parking when ADU within 1/2 mile of transit.

title: Driveway Approach Standards
text: Residential driveway approach typically 10-20 feet wide; commercial 24-30 feet. Maximum 30-40 feet in most jurisdictions without exception. Located minimum 5 feet from property line, 10 feet from a fire hydrant, and outside the visibility triangle at intersections. Curb cut requires an encroachment permit from public works. ADA cross-slope across sidewalk through driveway 2 percent maximum.

title: Sidewalk Requirements
text: Public sidewalks shall be minimum 4 feet wide in residential areas, 5-6 feet in commercial. Concrete minimum 4 inches thick on 4-inch compacted aggregate base. ADA running slope maximum 5 percent (or follows street grade), cross slope maximum 2 percent. Damaged sidewalk panels adjacent to a project site typically must be replaced as a condition of permit.

title: Grading Permit Thresholds
text: Grading permit typically required when cut or fill exceeds 50 cubic yards, slope height exceeds 5 feet, or excavation is within a designated hillside or hazard area. Engineered grading required above 200 cy or where slopes steeper than 2:1 are proposed. Geotechnical report typically required for engineered grading and for any retaining wall over 4 feet (including footing depth).

title: Construction Stormwater Permit
text: Construction sites disturbing one or more acres of soil, or smaller sites that are part of a common plan of development that totals one or more acres, must obtain coverage under the NPDES Construction General Permit and prepare a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP). California sites also obtain coverage under the State CGP (Order 2022-0057-DWQ). SWPPP must include BMPs for erosion, sediment, waste management, and post-construction stormwater quality.

title: Lead-Safe Work Practices
text: The EPA Renovation, Repair, and Painting (RRP) Rule requires that firms performing renovation, repair, and painting projects that disturb lead-based paint in pre-1978 homes, child care facilities, and pre-schools be certified by EPA, use certified renovators, follow specific work practices, and provide the EPA Renovate Right pamphlet to owners and tenants. Triggers: disturbing more than 6 square feet of interior or 20 square feet of exterior painted surface, or window replacement.
